title: 2D6 Dungeon - for once I'm excited about the combat in a dungeon crawler
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WNhZUBZ8tUc

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens the video by talking about combat — something he almost never does — because Toby Lancaster's *2D6 Dungeon* (then in Kickstarter playtest) has, to his eye, one of the best combat systems he has seen in a tabletop game. The mechanism: pick a weapon and two manoeuvres, each requiring a specific 2d6 combo (e.g. 6+4, 3+3). You roll your primary and secondary die in different colours, then spend Shift points to nudge each die up or down by one — but 1s and 6s can only move inward, making the extremes harder to hit. Each enemy has an Interrupt that triggers on specific secondary-die values, mitigating your damage. A Combat Fatigue die ticks up each round, eventually granting both sides bonus Shift as everyone tires and openings appear.

Around the combat sits a generous random-table engine: a 20×20 grid, 2d6 room dimensions (doubles cascade), d66 room contents, six gods, layered armour and door tables, between-dungeon Tavern and Market events, and an ancestry-themed scaling structure across ten dungeons. The bestiary system has interrupts and shift baked in. Daniel is openly jealous he didn't design the combat.

----

Does the shift-and-interrupt mechanism in 2D6 Dungeon actually deliver the tactical-but-fast feel it promises, or does the bookkeeping eat the speed?
